articles.title: It's dead, Jim! (UEFI CA expiry)
articles.summary: The Microsoft UEFI CA from 2011 has expired and is no longer valid for use with software signed using its root certificate. This means that any shim binaries created before the expiration date are now considered invalid, requiring new shim binaries to be generated in their place. The Debian community was able to quickly create dual-signed shim binaries using existing shim-review team members, which have been accepted and signed by Microsoft.

The author notes that this behavior does not seem to be commutative, as it breaks the usual logical flow of the language.

articles.title: Data Access Patterns That Makes Your CPU Really Angry
articles.summary: The core topic revolves around optimizing data access patterns to minimize CPU usage by exploiting memory pitfalls. The key technology in focus is a specific data access pattern that sums numbers as slowly as possible, which can be achieved by using a fixed accumulation function with a random position selection method. This approach allows for significant performance degradation compared to a randomized access pattern.

articles.title: Excessive nil pointer checks in Go
articles.summary: Go developers are advised to be cautious when using the nil pointer check, as excessive usage can lead to performance issues and potential crashes. The issue arises from the fact that Go's type system does not guarantee the absence of a value at runtime, making it necessary for developers to explicitly check for nil before accessing its fields or methods. This can result in unnecessary allocations and garbage collection, especially when working with large datasets. As such, developers should carefully consider their use of nil pointer checks to optimize performance and memory usage.

articles.title: Engineering for Bounded Cognition
articles.summary: The mind that builds software is far smaller than the software it builds, and almost everything good in engineering is a way of living with this gap. 3 February 2026 cross-cutting You've probably heard that the mind can hold seven things at once. It's the most repeated figure in popular psychology, but George Miller called his 1956 paper "The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two" half as a joke. He stripped away the tricks used to cheat and found that the honest figure for how many separate things a person can hold in mind at once is about four. This ratio holds true even when software is built with hundreds of millions of lines of code, making it a surprising thing about engineering that breaks down under pressure.
